bibliographic_data.title: A Neural Temporal Model for Human Motion Prediction
bibliographic_data.abstract: We propose novel neural temporal models for predicting and synthesizing human motion, achieving state-of-the-art in modeling long-term motion trajectories while being competitive with prior work in short-term prediction and requiring significantly less computation. Key aspects of our proposed system include: 1) a novel, two-level processing architecture that aids in generating planned trajectories, 2) a simple set of easily computable features that integrate derivative information, and 3) a novel multi-objective loss function that helps the model to slowly progress from simple next-step prediction to the harder task of multi-step, closed-loop prediction. Our results demonstrate that these innovations improve the modeling of long-term motion trajectories. Finally, we propose a novel metric, called Normalized Power Spectrum Similarity (NPSS), to evaluate the long-term predictive ability of motion synthesis models, complementing the popular mean-squared error (MSE) measure of Euler joint angles over time.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper proposes a novel two-level processing architecture, easily computable features integrating derivative information, and a multi-objective loss function for improving long-term human motion prediction.
core_content.key_methodology: A neural temporal model with a two-stage processing architecture that includes generating planned trajectories, using derivative information as features, and employing a multi-objective loss function to balance next-step and long-term predictions.
outcomes: Achieved state-of-the-art in modeling long-term motion trajectories
Competitive performance in short-term prediction with less computation
Proposed Normalized Power Spectrum Similarity (NPSS) as a novel metric for evaluating long-term motion synthesis
core_content.full_text_markdown: The paper addresses the problem of building predictive models of human movement using motion capture data. It proposes a neural temporal model that includes a two-level processing architecture, derivative information features, and a multi-objective loss function to improve both short-term and long-term predictions. The authors introduce a novel metric called Normalized Power Spectrum Similarity (NPSS) for evaluating the quality of long-term human motion synthesis models. Experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of these innovations in modeling long-term motion trajectories more accurately than previous methods.
